---
title: "Chronos-2"
subtitle: "From zero-shot to agent-tuned foundation model forecasting"
---

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
metrics = FileAttachment("_data/combined_metrics.json").json()
```

## What is Chronos-2?

Chronos-2 is a **time series foundation model** by Amazon, pretrained on billions of time series observations from diverse domains. It produces probabilistic forecasts by tokenizing time series values and using a transformer architecture.

We use **Chronos-2 Bolt Small** (~20M parameters) via AutoGluon TimeSeries, which provides a clean interface for both zero-shot inference and fine-tuning.

### The pipeline the agent controls

```
Available data (at forecast origin)
    ↓
Covariate selection ← agent chooses
    ↓
Data transformations ← agent chooses (log_diff, pct_change, etc.)
    ↓
Context window ← agent sets length
    ↓
Chronos-2 model (architecture FIXED)
    ↓
Fine-tuning settings ← agent chooses (on/off, LR, steps)
    ↓
Probabilistic forecast → point forecast (mean)
```

## Zero-shot performance

With no covariates and no fine-tuning, Chronos-2 relies purely on the shape of each target series to forecast:

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: fig-zs-vs-arima
//| fig-cap: "Zero-shot Chronos-2 vs ARIMA across target variables (RMSE, validation era)."

{
  const selected = metrics.methods.filter(m =>
    ["arima", "chronos2_zs"].includes(m.name)
  );

  const vars = ["cpi", "industrial_production", "retail_sales", "unemployment"];
  const data = selected.flatMap(m =>
    vars.flatMap(v =>
      Object.entries(m.metrics[v] || {}).map(([h, vals]) => ({
        method: m.display_name,
        variable: v.replace(/_/g, " ").replace(/\b\w/g, c => c.toUpperCase()),
        horizon: +h,
        rmse: vals.rmse
      }))
    )
  );

  return Plot.plot({
    height: 400,
    marginLeft: 60,
    marginBottom: 50,
    facet: {data, x: "variable"},
    x: {label: "Horizon", ticks: [1, 3, 6, 12]},
    y: {label: "RMSE", grid: true},
    fx: {label: null},
    marks: [
      Plot.line(data, {
        x: "horizon", y: "rmse", stroke: "method",
        strokeWidth: 2, fx: "variable", curve: "monotone-x"
      }),
      Plot.dot(data, {
        x: "horizon", y: "rmse", fill: "method",
        r: 3, fx: "variable", tip: true
      })
    ]
  });
}
```

**Observation:** Chronos-2 zero-shot is competitive with ARIMA at h=1 for most variables, but degrades at longer horizons. This suggests the model captures short-term dynamics but lacks the economy-specific knowledge needed for multi-step forecasting.

## The foundation model ladder

The research tests a three-way decomposition of forecast improvement:

1. **Foundation model effect:** Does Chronos-2 zero-shot beat classical methods?
2. **Fine-tuning effect:** Does economy-specific training improve over zero-shot?
3. **Agentic search effect:** Does LLM-guided search beat manual pipeline choices?

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: fig-ladder
//| fig-cap: "Average RMSE at h=12 — the foundation model ladder. Lower is better."

{
  // Build the ladder data from available methods
  const horizonKey = "12";
  const ladderMethods = [
    {name: "random_walk", label: "Naive\n(Random Walk)", step: 0},
    {name: "arima", label: "Classical\n(ARIMA)", step: 1},
    {name: "chronos2_zs", label: "Chronos-2\n(Zero-shot)", step: 2},
  ];

  const data = ladderMethods.map(lm => {
    const m = metrics.methods.find(mm => mm.name === lm.name);
    return {
      label: lm.label,
      step: lm.step,
      rmse: m ? (m.summary[horizonKey]?.avg_rmse || 0) : 0
    };
  }).filter(d => d.rmse > 0);

  return Plot.plot({
    height: 300,
    marginLeft: 60,
    marginBottom: 80,
    x: {label: null, domain: data.map(d => d.label), padding: 0.3},
    y: {label: "Average RMSE (h=12)", grid: true, domain: [0, 3.5]},
    marks: [
      Plot.barY(data, {
        x: "label", y: "rmse",
        fill: d => ["#95a5a6", "#2ecc71", "#9b59b6"][d.step],
        tip: true
      }),
      Plot.text(data, {
        x: "label", y: "rmse", text: d => d.rmse.toFixed(2),
        dy: -10, fontSize: 13, fontWeight: "bold"
      })
    ]
  });
}
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## What comes next
The bars for **Fine-tuned** and **Agent-tuned** Chronos-2 will appear here once those experiments are run. The search loop (`search.py`) automates the agent-tuned experiments.
:::

## Search space

The agent can explore these dimensions:

| Dimension | Range | Notes |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| Covariates | 0-10 from 14 available | Oil, FX, rates, global macro |
| Transforms | 7 options per variable | log_diff, pct_change, standardize, MA |
| Context length | 24-128 months or unlimited | How far back to look |
| Fine-tuning | On/off | Economy-specific training |
| Learning rate | 1e-5 to 1e-3 | If fine-tuning |
| Fine-tune steps | 50-500 | If fine-tuning |
| Grouping | Univariate or joint | One model per target vs shared |

This creates a large combinatorial space that manual tuning cannot exhaustively explore.
